steps.title: Set up the inequality for when a monthly pass saves money
steps.content:
To find the minimum number of trips where a monthly pass becomes cheaper, assume all trips cost the maximum price (3.25 dollars each). For the pass to cost less than individual tickets:
$$92 \lt n \times 3.25$$
where $n$ is the number of trips.

steps.title: Solve for the minimum number of trips
steps.content:
Divide both sides by $3.25$:
$$n \gt \frac{92}{3.25}$$
Rewrite the fraction:
$$n \gt \frac{9200}{325} = \frac{368}{13}$$
Calculate: $368 \div 13 = 28\frac{4}{13} \approx 28.31$

Since $n$ must be a whole number and $n \gt 28.31$, the minimum is $n = 29$.

Verification: 29 trips at 3.25 dollars each cost 94.25 dollars, which exceeds the 92-dollar monthly pass. Therefore, the answer is 29.
